burial.plot_notes: Saint Martin's Episcopal Church Memorial Garden, Radnor PA — alongside wife Anne Skerrett.

notable.notable_blurb: Federal Trade Commission Chairman 1970-1973, Nixon appointee

parents.mother_name: (William Huntington Kirkpatrick's wife — name not documented)
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother's name not in currently-available sources. Possibly documented in private Kirkpatrick family records. To be researched.

career.notes: Partner at Morgan, Lewis & Bockius — a major Philadelphia law firm. Antitrust specialist. Retired from the firm 1985.
Head of the American Bar Association's antitrust section 1968-1969.

career.organization: ABA Commission to Study the FTC
career.notes: At Nixon's request 1969, led the ABA commission to look into the FTC. The commission concluded that many high-level FTC employees were incompetent or do-nothings and that the agency was pursuing trivial matters. Nixon impressed.

Federal Trade Commission Chairman September 15, 1970 - February 20, 1973. Appointed by President Nixon after Caspar Weinberger (initial Nixon FTC chair) was moved to head OMB. Credited with the FTC's reorganization and dramatic improvements in functioning. Under his tenure FTC ordered Coca-Cola to stop claiming one of its drinks was abundant in vitamin C; ordered antifreeze Zerex to warn it could damage automotive cooling systems; prodded manufacturers to substantiate safety/performance/therapeutic claims. Resigned January 1973 (officially voluntary).
Active in community affairs post-retirement; board member of Solesbury School.

military_service.notes: US Army Air Corps WWII. Discharged for medical reasons 1944.

cross_connections.display_label: Earlier-generation American antitrust leader; Cadwalader Wickersham name partner

narrative_blocks.header: Chaired Federal Trade Commission for Nixon, 1970-1973
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed Federal Trade Commission Chairman by President Nixon September 1970 — credited with reorganizing the agency and dramatically improving its functioning. Under his tenure the commission ordered Coca-Cola to stop claiming a drink was abundant in vitamin C and forced antifreeze Zerex to warn about automotive cooling-system damage.

Headed the American Bar Association's antitrust section 1968-1969 and led the Nixon-requested ABA commission examining the FTC. The commission concluded too many high-level FTC employees were incompetent and the agency was pursuing trivial matters — Nixon was impressed and brought him in to fix it.

narrative_blocks.header: Resigned FTC chairmanship with four years remaining
narrative_blocks.body: Resigned January 1973 with almost four years remaining on his FTC term — officially voluntary but with indications Nixon was not displeased to see him go. Independent style and aggressive antitrust actions against large corporations had displeased segments of the administration. The Miles W.

burial.plot_notes: Saint Martin's Episcopal Church Memorial Garden, Radnor PA — alongside wife Anne Skerrett.

burial.plot_notes: Saint Martin's Episcopal Church Memorial Garden, Radnor PA — alongside husband Miles.

parents.father_research_notes: Skerrett family of Pennsylvania. Siblings: Jan Pierce (predeceased), Henry Skerrett (predeceased), Frances Grosholz (predeceased). No documented Hooker/Talcott connection.

career.organization: Chester County CARES / National Governors Association / local civic organizations
career.notes: Tireless volunteer for national and local civic organizations, including Chester County CARES and the National Governors Association.

military_service.unit: WAVES (Women Accepted for Volunteer Emergency Service) — US Navy
military_service.notes: Served in the WAVES — the Women Accepted for Volunteer Emergency Service, the US Navy's women's reserve, during World War II.

narrative_blocks.header: WWII WAVES officer married Miles fifty-three years
narrative_blocks.body: Served as a WAVES officer in the United States Navy during World War II — the Women Accepted for Volunteer Emergency Service reserve. Married Miles Wells Kirkpatrick for fifty-three years until his 1998 death; mother of three including daughter Anna 'Nan' Kirkpatrick Urban (ALS death 2009).

death.cause: ALS (Lou Gehrig's disease) after three-year battle; died at home surrounded by family.

education.notes: The Agnes Irwin School, Rosemont PA — Philadelphia-area girls' school descended from / related to Miss Irwin's School where her great-grandmother Margaret Carroll Jones Turnbull (TD0242) taught history a half-century earlier.
Graduated University of Colorado in Boulder CO.

education.school_name: University of Connecticut, School of Social Work
education.notes: Master's in Social Work — commuted weekly to Concord NH while raising children.

career.notes: School social worker at Deering High School Portland ME for nearly twenty-five years. Helped at every school in the Portland School System over the course of her career. Former students sent letters during her ALS battle recounting how her compassion had saved their lives.

narrative_blocks.header: Three-year ALS battle ended at home, 2009
narrative_blocks.body: Diagnosed with ALS — Lou Gehrig's disease — and battled the disease three years before dying at home in Portland Maine March 29, 2009 age 59, surrounded by her husband and four children. Her obituary characterized her as 'a casualty of this terrible disease and not a victim' — keeping her loving spirit alive until the end.

narrative_blocks.header: Deering High School social worker, twenty-five years
narrative_blocks.body: Worked as a social worker at Deering High School in Portland Maine for nearly twenty-five years — helping across every school in the Portland School System. After her ALS diagnosis became known she received countless letters from former students recounting how her compassion had saved their lives.

parents.father_research_notes: Lee was born and raised in Portland Maine per his late wife's 2009 obituary. Parents not yet researched. No documented Hooker/Talcott connection.

bio.extra_names: Named for paternal grandfather William Huntington Kirkpatrick (TD0249, Federal Judge)

burial.plot_notes: Christ Episcopal Church Cemetery, West River MD (Anne Arundel County) — interment per Wikipedia and Congressional Bioguide.

notable.notable_blurb: Federal Judge Eastern District PA 1927-1970, Coolidge appointee

education.notes: Attended the University of Pennsylvania Law School 1905-1906; admitted to the bar 1908.

career.notes: Practiced law at Easton from 1908; member of his father's firm Kirkpatrick & Maxwell after 1908 admission.
Republican to 67th US Congress March 4, 1921 - March 3, 1923. Unsuccessful 1922 reelection. Resumed Easton law practice 1923-1927.

career.organization: United States District Court, Eastern District of Pennsylvania
career.notes: Appointed by President Calvin Coolidge March 3, 1927 to seat created by 44 Stat. 1347. Chief Judge 1948-1958. Senior status May 1, 1958 - November 28, 1970. Last federal judge in active service appointed by Coolidge. Influential corporate/securities law decisions: Insurance Shares Corp. v. Northern Fiscal Corp. (controlling shareholder fiduciary duty not to sell to looters); Kardon v. National Gypsum Co. (first recognized implied private cause of action for SEC Rule 10b-5 violations).
Lafayette College trustee 1933-1961 — succeeding his father William Sebring on the board.

military_service.unit: Judge Advocate General's Corps, US Army
military_service.notes: Major and Lieutenant Colonel, Judge Advocate; member of the Board of Review of Courts-Martial, United States Army. Service in Washington DC.

institutions.institution_blurb: Lafayette College A.B. 1905 + trustee 1933-1961 — succeeded his father on the board.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Huntington' carries the Talcott-line Huntington surname forward via his mother's matronymic — her grandmother was Elizabeth Hale Huntington Jones.

cross_connections.display_label: Earlier Philadelphia District Court judge; Mayor of Philadelphia 1849

narrative_blocks.header: Coolidge-appointed Federal Judge for forty-three years
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed by President Calvin Coolidge March 3, 1927 to the US District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania and served until his death November 28, 1970. Chief Judge 1948-1958 and the last federal judge in active service appointed by Coolidge.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote foundational decisions in corporate securities law
narrative_blocks.body: Issued early but influential decisions in Insurance Shares Corporation v Northern Fiscal — establishing the controlling-shareholder fiduciary duty not to sell to looters. Also wrote Kardon v National Gypsum, the first decision recognizing an implied private cause of action for SEC Rule 10b-5 violations.

narrative_blocks.header: Judge Advocate General Corps, World War I
narrative_blocks.body: Served World War I as major and lieutenant colonel, judge advocate, and member of the Board of Review of Courts-Martial of the United States Army at Washington. Returned to Easton private practice after the war before his 1921 Congressional election.

notable.notable_blurb: Pennsylvania Attorney General 1887-1891 and US Congressman

parents.father_research_notes: Newton Kirkpatrick (1820-1863), born Ringoes NJ; teacher Easton public schools; pupil and assistant at Rev John Vanderveer DD academy; attended Lafayette College. Descended from David → Alexander → David → Alexander Kirkpatrick (the founder). Not built.
parents.mother_research_notes: Susan Sebring (1822-1909), daughter of Judge William L. Sebring of Easton — prominent Easton lawyer and source of son's middle name.

education.notes: Lafayette College A.B. class of 1863 — Easton PA. Studied classical course.

career.notes: Admitted to the Northampton County bar October 5, 1865 age 21. Practiced over fifty years; senior partner Kirkpatrick & Maxwell. Specialized in cases reaching state and federal supreme courts.
Solicitor of the city of Easton, gaining instant reputation as a young lawyer of promise.
Appointed President Judge of the Third Judicial District of Pennsylvania 1874-1875 — filled vacancy until elective term expired.

Lafayette faculty 1875-1877 (professor of municipal law); lecturer on municipal law subsequently. Board of Trustees 1890-1932 — last 42 years, vice president of the board. Acting president 1902-1903 while President Ethelbert Dudley Warfield recuperated in Europe.
Attorney General of Pennsylvania January 18, 1887 - January 1891 under Governor James A. Beaver.

Republican to the 55th US Congress March 4, 1897 - March 3, 1899. Won by 329 votes in an Eighth Pennsylvania District that had given uniform Democratic majorities of thousands. 1898 unsuccessful reelection.

institutions.institution_blurb: Lafayette College trustee 1890-1932 (42 years, vice president of board); A.B. 1863; faculty 1875-1877; acting president 1902-1903.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Sebring' honors maternal grandfather Judge William L. Sebring of Easton PA — prominent Northampton County lawyer.

narrative_blocks.body: Served as Attorney General of Pennsylvania under Governor James A. Beaver January 1887 to January 1891 — appointed at age 43 after the President Judge bench of the Third Judicial District. Previously solicitor of the city of Easton 1866-1874.

narrative_blocks.header: Won 329-vote House race in Democratic district
narrative_blocks.body: Elected to the 55th US Congress 1896 as Republican in the Eighth Pennsylvania District, where Democratic majorities had previously run into the thousands. The winning margin was 329 votes. Served one term to March 1899; lost the 1898 reelection.

narrative_blocks.header: Lafayette College acting president twice over
narrative_blocks.body: Twice held the temporary presidency of Lafayette College — most notably 1902-1903 while President Ethelbert Dudley Warfield recuperated in Europe. Trustee 1890-1932 (vice president of the board, 42 years), faculty 1875-1877, lecturer on municipal law, and 1863 alumnus.

bio.extra_names: Named for paternal grandmother Elizabeth Hale Huntington Jones

burial.plot_notes: Easton Cemetery, Easton PA — alongside husband William Sebring.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Huntington' carries the Talcott-line Huntington surname forward through her paternal grandmother Elizabeth Hale Huntington Jones.

narrative_blocks.header: Bore two notable sons one year apart
narrative_blocks.body: Bore William Huntington Kirkpatrick in October 1885 and Donald Morris Kirkpatrick in March 1887 — just seventeen months apart. The first would become a Federal Judge appointed by Calvin Coolidge, the second a Paris Prize Beaux-Arts architect; both Lafayette College graduates following their father onto its board.

INST023.name: Hopkins Academy
INST023.notes: Hadley, Massachusetts academy founded from the bequest of Edward Hopkins — the same bequest behind Hopkins Grammar School, New Haven (INST001), but a DISTINCT institution. Roger Hooker Leavitt and Hart Leavitt are graduates; compiler flagged the distinction Stage 6Y-687.

INST143.name: University of Pennsylvania
INST143.notes:
Founded 1740 Philadelphia by Benjamin Franklin. Private Ivy League research university. Schools of particular tree relevance: Perelman School of Medicine (1765, oldest medical school in US), Wharton School of Business (1881, first collegiate business school in US), Stuart Weitzman School of Design (architecture).

HOOKER-TREE CONNECTIONS — 13 across Hooker, Talcott, and spouse branches:

PACKARD MEDICAL DYNASTY (4 generations of physicians):
- H04335 Dr. Frederic Adolphus Packard (Penn Med)
- H02745 Dr. John Hooker Packard (Penn Med)
- H04337 Dr. Francis Randolph Packard (Penn Med)
- H04334 Charles Stuart Wood Packard

KIRKPATRICK BRANCH (Talcott line gen 8-10, three Lafayette-to-Penn transition figures):
- TD0249 William Huntington Kirkpatrick (Penn Law — also Lafayette A.B. 1905)
- TD0250 Donald Morris Kirkpatrick (Penn BS Architecture 1911 + Penn Christian Association leadership 1927-29 + Penn faculty 1931-34 + Paris Prize 1912 ninth recipient)
- TD0251 Miles Wells Kirkpatrick (Penn Law — Princeton BA + Penn Law; later ABA Antitrust Chair + FTC Chairman 1970-73)

TURNBULL BRANCH (Talcott gen 9-10):
- TD0242 Margaret Carroll Jones Turnbull (Penn)
- TD0248 Elizabeth Huntington Jones Kirkpatrick (Penn Christian Association connection via husband I01140 William Sebring + son TD0250 Donald)

OTHER:
- HD3384 Michael Gay Hooker
- TD0115 Michael Page (Wharton School of Business)
- X00641 Jonathan Huntington Lyman Jr. (Penn Med)
- X02844 Bancel La Farge (Penn Med)

Penn's location in Philadelphia made it the natural medical-school destination for both the Hooker-Packard physicians and the Jones-Kirkpatrick lawyers who moved to Philadelphia after Joel Jones's 1834 relocation from Easton.
